sourceMovie.title: Cube
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: Audiences latched onto Cube (1998) as a thriller theatrical experience: big enough for a group watch, specific enough to reward a second viewing once you know where it's headed. The thriller and science fiction blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: The Maze Runner
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Cube clicked for you, The Maze Runner (2014) is a strong next stop: both trade in thriller and science fiction, with momentum and clean stakes. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: 10 Cloverfield Lane
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Cube clicked for you, 10 Cloverfield Lane (2016) is a strong next stop: both trade in thriller and science fiction, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Cube clicked for you, Escape Room (2019) is a strong next stop: both trade in thriller and mystery, with unease that lingers after the credits. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Cube?
faq.answer: Try The Maze Runner, 10 Cloverfield Lane, Escape Room—each shares DNA with Cube while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Cube?
faq.answer: Cube (1998) is best described as Thriller, Science Fiction, Mystery. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Cube part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Cube Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
